The powers $6 ^ { k }$ and $1 ^ { k }$ appear in that last matrix $A ^ { k } ,$ mixed in by the eigenvectors.

For the difference equation $u _ { k + 1 } = A u _ { k } ,$ we emphasize the main point. Every eigen-
vector $x$ produces a "pure solution" with powers of $2 :$

One solution is $u _ { 0 } = x , \quad u _ { 1 } = \lambda x , \quad u _ { 2 } = \lambda ^ { 2 } x , \ldots .$

When the initial $u _ { 0 }$ is an eigenvector x, this is the solution: $u _ { k } = \lambda ^ { k } x .$ In general $u _ { 0 }$
is not an eigenvector. But if $u _ { 0 }$ is a combination of eigenvectors, the solution $u _ { k }$ is the
same combination of these special solutions.

5H If $u _ { 0 } = c _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdots + c _ { n } x _ { n } ,$ then after $k$ steps $u _ { k } = c _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { k } x _ { 1 } + \cdots + c _ { n } \lambda _ { n } ^ { k } x _ { n }$
Choose $c ^ { \prime } s t c$ match the starting vector $u _ { 0 } :$

$$u _ { 0 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { } & { } & { } \\ { } & { } & { } & { x _ { n } } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { } _ { c _ { 1 } } \\ { \vdots } \\ { } ^ { c } \end{array} \right] = S c \quad \text { and } \quad c = S ^ { - 1 } u _ { 0 } .$$

## Markov Matrices

There was an exercise in Chapter 1, about moving in and out of California, that is worth
another look. These were the rules:

Each year $\frac { 1 } { 1 0 }$ of the people outside California move in, and $\frac { 2 } { 1 0 }$ of the people
inside California move out. We start with yo people outside and $z o$ inside.

At the end of the first year the numbers outside and inside are $y _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 1 } :$


<table>
<tr>
<td>Difference</td>
<td>$$\left. \begin{array}{} { y _ { 1 } = . 9 y _ { 0 } + . 2 z _ { 0 } } & { \text { or } } & { y _ { 1 } } \\ { z _ { 1 } = . 1 y _ { 0 } + . 8 z _ { 0 } } & { \text { ze } } & { z _ { 1 } } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { . 9 } & { . 2 } \\ { . 1 } & { . 8 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { y _ { 0 } } \\ { z _ { 0 } } \end{array} \right]$$</td>
<td></td>

This problem and its matrix have the two essential properties of a Markov process:

1\. The total number of people stays fixed: Each column of the Markov matrix adds
up to 1. Nobody is gained or lost.

2\. The numbers outside and inside can never become negative: The matrix has $n o$
negative entries. The powers $A ^ { k }$ are all nonnegative.3

We solve this Markov difference equation using $u _ { k } = S \Lambda ^ { k } S ^ { - 1 } u _ { 0 } .$ Then we show that
the population approaches a "steady state." First $A$ has to be diagonalized:

$$A - 2 I = \left[ \begin{array}{} . 9 - \lambda & . 2 \\ . 1 & . 8 - \lambda \end{array} \right] \quad \mathrm { h a s } \quad \mathrm { d e t } \left( A - \lambda I \right) = \lambda ^ { 2 } - 1 . 7 \lambda + . 7$$

3Furthermore, history is completely disregarded; each new $u _ { k + 1 }$ depends only on the current $u _ { k } .$ Perhaps even
our lives are examples of Markov processes, but I hope not.

<!-- PageNumber="287" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.3 Difference Equations and Powers $A ^ { k }$" -->

$\mathrm { d } \lambda _ { 2 } = . 7 : \quad A = S \Lambda S ^ { - 1 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} \frac { 2 } { 3 } & \frac { 1 } { 3 } \\ \frac { 1 } { 3 } & - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} 1 & & 1 \\ 1 & & 1 \\ 1 & - 2 \end{array} \right] .$

.

To find $A ^ { k } ,$ and the distribution after k years, change SAS-1 to $S \Lambda ^ { k } S :$

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { y _ { k } } \\ { z _ { k } } \end{array} \right] = A ^ { k } \left[ \begin{array}{} { y _ { 0 } } \\ { z _ { 0 } } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } & { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \\ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } & { - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { y _ { 0 } } \\ { z _ { 0 } } \end{array} \right]$$
$$= \left( y _ { 0 } + z _ { 0 } \right) \left[ \begin{array}{} \frac { 2 } { 3 } \\ \frac { 1 } { 3 } \end{array} \right] + \left( y _ { 0 } - 2 z _ { 0 } \right) \left( . 7 \right) ^ { k } \left[ \begin{array}{} \frac { 1 } { 3 } \\ - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \end{array} \right] .$$

Those two terms are $c _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { k } x _ { 1 } + c _ { 2 } \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { k } x _ { 2 } .$ The factor $2 _ { 1 } ^ { k } = 1$ is hidden in the first term. In
the long run, the other factor $\left( . 7 \right) ^ { k }$ becomes extremely small. The solution approaches
a limiting state $u _ { \infty } = \left( y _ { \infty } , z _ { \infty } \right) :$

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { y _ { \infty } } \\ { z _ { \infty } } \end{array} \right] = \left( y _ { 0 } + z _ { 0 } \right) \left[ \begin{array}{} { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \end{array} \right]$$

The total population is still $y _ { 0 } + z _ { 0 } ,$ but in the limit $\frac { 2 } { 3 }$ of this population is outside Cali-
fornia and $\frac { 1 } { 3 }$ is inside. This is true no matter what the initial distribution may have been!
If the year starts with $\frac { 2 } { 3 }$ outside and $\frac { 1 } { 3 }$ inside, then it ends the same way:

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { . 9 } & { . 2 } \\ { . 1 } & { . 8 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } \\ { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \end{array} \right] . \quad \text { or } \quad A u _ { \infty } = u _ { \infty } .$$

The steady state is the eigenvector of $A$ corresponding to $2 = 1 .$ Multiplication by $A ,$
from one time step to the next, leaves $\mathcal{U} _ { \infty }$ unchanged.

The theory of Markov processes is illustrated by that California example:

51 A Markov matrix $A$ has all $a _ { i j } \geq 0 ,$ with each column adding to 1.

(a) $\lambda _ { 1 } = 1$ is an eigenvalue of $A .$

(b) Its eigenvector $x _ { 1 }$ is nonnegative-and it is a steady state, since $A x _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } .$

(c) The other eigenvalues satisfy $\| \lambda _ { i } \| \leq 1 .$

(d) If $A$ or any power of $A$ has all positive entries, these other $| 2 _ { i } |$ are below 1.
The solution $A ^ { k } u _ { 0 }$ approaches a multiple of x1-which is the steady state
$u _ { \infty } .$

To find the right multiple of $x _ { 1 } ,$ use the fact that the total population stays the same. If
California started with all 90 million people out, it ended with 60 million out and 30
million in. It ends the same way if all 90 million were originally inside.

We note that many authors transpose the matrix so its rows add to 1.

Remark. Our description of a Markov process was deterministic: populations moved in
fixed proportions. But if we look at a single individual, the fractions that move become

1

3

1
3

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="288" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 5 Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors" -->

probabilities. With probability $\frac { 1 } { 1 0 } ,$ an individual outside California moves in. If inside,
the probability of moving out is $\frac { 2 } { 1 0 } .$ The movement becomes a random process, and $A$ is
called a transition matrix.

The components of $u _ { k } = A ^ { k } u _ { 0 }$ specify the probability that the individual is outside
or inside the state. These probabilities are never negative and add to 1-everybody has
to be somewhere. That brings us back to the two fundamental properties of a Markov
matrix: Each column adds to 1, and no entry is negative.

Why is $2 = 1$ always an eigenvalue? Each column of $A \quad - \quad I$ adds up to $1 \quad - \quad 1 = 0 .$
Therefore the rows of $A \quad - \quad I$ add up to the zero row, they are linearly dependent, and
$\det \left( A - I \right) = 0 .$

Except for very special cases, $u _ { k }$ will approach the corresponding eigenvector4. In
the formula $u _ { k } = c _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { k } x _ { 1 } + \cdots + c _ { n } \lambda _ { n } ^ { k } x _ { n } ,$ no eigenvalue can be larger than 1. (Otherwise
the probabilities $u _ { k }$ would blow up.) If all other eigenvalues are strictly smaller than
$\lambda _ { 1 } = 1 ,$ then the first term in the formula will be dominant. The other $A _ { i } ^ { k } ,$ go to zero, and
$u _ { k } \rightarrow c _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } = u _ { \infty } = \text { steady } s$ state.

This is an example of one of the central themes of this chapter: Given information
about $A ,$ find information about its eigenvalues. Here we found $A _ { \max } = 1 .$


### Stability of $u _ { k + 1 } = A u _ { k }$

There is an obvious difference between Fibonacci numbers and Markov processes. The
numbers $F _ { k }$ become larger and larger, while by definition any "probability" is between 0
and 1. The Fibonacci equation is unstable. So is the compound interest equation $P _ { k + 1 } =$
$1 . 0 6 P _ { k }$ the principal keeps growing forever. If the Markov probabilities decreased to
zero, that equation would be stable; but they do not, since at every stage they must add
to 1. Therefore a Markov process is neutrally stable.

We want to study the behavior of $u _ { k + 1 } = A u _ { k }$ as $k \rightarrow \infty .$ Assuming that $A$ can be
diagonalized, $u _ { k }$ will be a combination of pure solutions:

Solution at time k uk = SAKSuo = C12 x1 + ... + Cnakxn.

The growth of $u _ { k }$ is governed by the $2 _ { i } ^ { k } .$ Stability depends on the eigenvalues:

5J The difference equation $u _ { k + 1 } = A u _ { k }$ is

stable if all eigenvalues satisfy $| 2 _ { i } | < 1$
neutrally stable if some | $| A _ { i } | = 1$ and all the other | $| \lambda _ { i } | < 1$ and
unstable if at least one eigenvalue has $| 2 _ { i } | > 1 .$

In the stable case, the powers $A ^ { k }$ approach zero and so does $u _ { k } = A ^ { k } u _ { 0 } .$

4If everybody outside moves in and everybody inside moves out, then the populations are reversed every year
and there is no steady state. The transition matrix is $A = \left[ \begin{array}{} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array} \right]$ and -1 is an eigenvalue as well $\mathrm { a s } + 1 - \mathrm { w h i c h }$
cannot happen if all $a _ { i i } > 0 .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="289" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.3 Difference Equations and Powers $A ^ { k }$" -->

Example 1. This matrix $A$ is certainly stable:

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 4 } \\ { 0 } & { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \end{array} \right] \quad \text { has eigenvalues } 0 \text { and } \frac { 1 } { 2 }$$

The A's are on the main diagonal because $A$ is triangular. Starting from any $u _ { 0 } ,$ and
following the rule $u _ { k + 1 } = A u _ { k } ,$ the solution must eventually approach zero:

$$u _ { 0 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} 0 \\ 1 \end{array} \right] , \quad u _ { 1 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} 4 \\ \frac { 1 } { 2 } \end{array} \right] , \quad u _ { 2 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} 2 \\ \frac { 1 } { 4 } \end{array} \right] , \quad u _ { 3 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} 1 \\ \frac { 1 } { 8 } \end{array} \right] , \quad u _ { 4 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \\ \frac { 1 } { 1 6 } \end{array} \right] , \cdots .$$

The larger eigenvalue $2 = \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ governs the decay; after the first step every $u _ { k }$ is $\frac { 1 } { 2 } u _ { k - 1 } .$ The
real effect of the first step is to split $u _ { 0 }$ into the two eigenvectors of $A :$

$$u _ { 0 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 8 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 8 } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] \text { and then } u _ { k } = \left( \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right) ^ { k } \left[ \begin{array}{} { 8 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] + \left( 0 \right) ^ { k } \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 8 } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right]$$


## Positive Matrices and Applications in Economics

By developing the Markov ideas we can find a small gold mine (entirely optional) of
matrix applications in economics.


### Example 2 (Leontief's input-output matrix).

This is one of the first great successes of mathematical economics. To illustrate it, we
construct a consumption matrix-in which $a _ { i j } ,$ gives the amount of product $j$ that is
needed to create one unit of product $\dot { l }$ i:

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { . 4 } & { 0 } & { . 1 } \\ { 0 } & { . 1 } & { . 8 } \\ { . 5 } & { . 7 } & { . 1 } \end{array} \right]$$
(steel)
.
(food)
(labor)

The first question is: Can we produce $y _ { 1 }$ units of steel, $y _ { 2 }$ units of food, and $y _ { 3 }$ units of
labor? We must start with larger amounts $p _ { 1 } ,$ $p _ { 2 } ,$ $p _ { 3 } ,$ because some part is consumed
by the production itself. The amount consumed is $A p ,$ and it leaves a net production of
$p - A p .$

Problem To find a vector $p$ such that $p - \quad A p = y ,$ or $p = \left( I - A \right) ^ { - 1 } y .$

On the surface, we are only asking if $I \quad - \quad A$ is invertible. But there is a nonnegative twist
to the problem. Demand and production, $y$ and $p ,$ are nonnegative. Since $p$ is $\left( 1 - A \right) ^ { - 1 } y ,$
the real question is about the matrix that multiplies $y :$

$$\left( I - A \right) ^ { - 1 }$$

Roughly speaking, $A$ cannot be too large. If production consumes too much, nothing is
left as output. The key is in the largest eigenvalue $2 1$ of $A ,$ which must be below 1:

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="290" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 5 Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors" -->

If $2 1 >$ 1, $\left( I - A \right) ^ { - 1 }$ fails to be nonnegative.

If $2 _ { 1 } = 1 ,$ $\left( I - A \right) ^ { - 1 }$ fails to exist.

If $2 1 < 1 ,$ $\left( I - A \right) ^ { - 1 }$ is a converging sum of nonnegative matrices:


## Geometric series

$$\left( I - A \right) ^ { - 1 } = I + A + A ^ { 2 } + A ^ { 3 } + \cdots .$$
(7)

The 3 by 3 example has $\lambda _ { 1 } = . 9 ,$ and output exceeds input. Production can go on.

Those are easy to prove, once we know the main fact about a nonnegative matrix $\mathrm { l i k e }$
$A :$ Not only is the largest eigenvalue $2 1$ positive, but so is the eigenvector $x _ { 1 } .$ Then
$\left( I - A \right) - 1$ has the same eigenvector, with eigenvalue $1 / \left( 1 - 2 _ { 1 } \right) .$

If $\lambda _ { 1 }$ exceeds 1, that last number is negative. The matrix $\left( I - A \right) ^ { - 1 }$ will take the
positive vector $x _ { 1 }$ to a negative vector $x _ { 1 } / \left( 1 - \lambda _ { 1 } \right) .$ In that case $\left( I - A \right) ^ { - 1 }$ is definitely
not nonnegative. If $\lambda _ { 1 } = 1$ 1, then $I \quad - \quad A$ is singular. The productive case is $2 _ { 1 } < 1 ,$ when
the powers of $A$ go to zero (stability) and the infinite series $I + A + A ^ { 2 } + \cdots .$ converges.
Multiplying this series by $I \quad - \quad A$ leaves the identity matrix-all higher powers cancel-so
$\left( I - A \right) ^ { - 1 }$ is a sum of nonnegative matrices, We give two examples:

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 2 } \\ { 2 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] \quad \text { has } \lambda _ { 1 } = 2 \text { and the economy is } \mathrm { l o s t }$$
$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} . 5 & 2 \\ 0 & . 5 \end{array} \right] \quad \mathrm { h a s } \lambda _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \text { and we can produce anything. } .$$

The matrices $\left( I - A \right) - 1$ in those two cases are $- \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$ and $\left[ \begin{array}{} { 2 } & { 8 } \\ { 0 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] .$

Leontief's inspiration was to find a model that uses genuine data from the real econ-
omy. The table for 1958 contained 83 industries in the United States, with a "trans-
actions table" of consumption and production for each one. The theory also reaches
beyond $\left( I - A \right) ^ { - 1 } ,$ to decide natural prices and questions of optimization. Normally la-
bor is in limited $s u p p l y$ and ought to be minimized. And, of course, the economy is not
always linear.


### Example 3 (The prices in a closed input-output model ).

The model is called "closed" when everything produced is also consumed. Nothing goes
outside the system. In that case $A$ goes back to a Markov matrix. The columns add up
to 1. We might be talking about the value of steel and food and labor, instead of the
number of units, The vector $p$ represents prices instead of production levels.

Suppose $p _ { 0 }$ is a vector of prices. Then $A p _ { 0 }$ multiplies prices by amounts to give the
value of each product. That is a new set of prices which the system uses for the next
set of values $A ^ { 2 } p _ { 0 } .$ The question is whether the prices approach equilibrium. Are there
prices such that $p = A p ,$ and does the system take us there?

You recognize $p$ as the (nonnegative) eigenvector of the Markov matrix $A ,$ with $2 =$ 1.
It is the steady state $p _ { \infty } ,$ and it is approached from any starting point $p _ { 0 } .$ By repeating a
transaction over and over, the price tends to equilibrium.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="291" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.3 Difference Equations and Powers $A ^ { k }$" -->

The "Perron-Frobenius theorem" gives the key properties of a positive matrix-not
to be confused with a positive definite matrix, which is symmetric and has all its eigen-
values positive. Here all the entries $a _ { i j }$ are positive.

5K If $A$ is a positive matrix, so is its largest eigenvalue: $\lambda _ { 1 } > \mathrm { a l l }$ other $| x _ { i } | .$

Every component of the corresponding eigenvector $x _ { 1 }$ is also positive.

Proof. Suppose $A > 0 .$ The key $i d e a$ is to look at all numbers $t$ such that $A x \geq t x$ for
some nonnegative vector $x$ (other than $x =$ 0). We are allowing inequality in $A x \geq t x$ in
order $\mathrm { t c }$ have many positive candidates t. For the largest value $t _ { \max }$ (which is attained),
we will show that equality holds: $A x = t _ { \max } x .$

Otherwise, if $A x \geq t _ { \max } x$ is not an equality, multiply by $A .$ Because $A \quad i s \quad p o s i t i v e ,$ that
produces a strict inequality $A ^ { 2 } x > t _ { \max } A x .$ Therefore the positive vector $y = A x$ satisfies
$A y > t _ { \max } y ,$ and $t _ { \max }$ could have been larger. This contradiction forces the equality $A x =$
$t _ { \max } x ,$ and we have an eigenvalue. Its eigenvector $x$ is positive (not just nonnegative)
because on the left-hand side of that equality $A x$ is sure to be positive.

To see that no eigenvalue can be larger than $t _ { \max } ,$ suppose $A z = A z .$ Since $2$ and $z$
may involve negative or complex numbers, we take absolute values: $| A | | z | = | A z | \leq A | z$
by the "triangle inequality." This $| z |$ is a nonnegative vector, so $| 2 |$ is one of the possible
candidates $t$ t. Therefore $| 2 |$ cannot exceed $2 1 ,$ which was $t _ { \max } .$

### Example 4 (Von Neumann's model of an expanding economy ).

We go back to the 3 by 3 matrix A that gave the consumption of steel, food, and labor.
If the outputs are $s 1 ,$ $f _ { 1 } ,$ $\ell _ { 1 } ,$ then the required inputs are

$$u _ { 0 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { . 4 } & { 0 } & { . 1 } \\ { 0 } & { . 1 } & { . 8 } \\ { . 5 } & { . 7 } & { . 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { s _ { 1 } } \\ { f _ { 1 } } \\ { l _ { 1 } } \end{array} \right] = A u _ { 1 } .$$

In economics the difference equation is backward! Instead of $u _ { 1 } = A u _ { 0 }$ we have $u _ { 0 } =$
$A u _ { 1 } .$ If $A$ is small (as it is), then production does not consume everything-and the
economy can grow. The eigenvalues of $A ^ { - 1 }$ will govern this growth. But again there is
a nonnegative twist, since steel, food, and labor cannot come in negative amounts. Von
Neumann asked for the maximum rate $t$ at which the economy can expand and $s t i l l \quad s t a y$
nonnegative, meaning that $u _ { 1 } \geq t u _ { 0 } \geq 0 .$

Thus the problem requires $u _ { 1 } \geq t A u _ { 1 } .$ It is like the Perron-Frobenius theorem, with $A$
on the other side. As before, equality holds when $t$ reaches tmax-which is the eigenvalue
associated with the positive eigenvector of $A ^ { - 1 } .$ In this example the expansion factor is
$\frac { 1 0 } { 9 } .$

9

$$x = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { 5 } \\ { 5 } \end{array} \right]$$
$$A x = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 4 } & { 0 } & { . 1 } \\ { 0 } & { . 1 } & { . 5 } \\ { . 5 } & { . 7 } & { . 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 . 9 } \\ { 4 . 5 } \\ { 4 . 5 } \end{array} \right.$$

x.

With steel-food-labor in the ratio $1 - 5 - 5 ,$ the economy grows as quickly as possible: The
maximum growth rate is $1 / 2 1 .$

year." They survive the first year with probability $\frac { 1 } { 2 } ,$ and the second with probability
$\frac { 1 } { 3 } ,$ and then produce six females on the way out:


## Beetle matrix

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 0 } & { 6 } \\ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]$$

Show that $A ^ { 3 } = I ,$ and follow the distribution of 3000 beetles for six years.

3\. For the Fibonacci matrix $A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] ,$ compute $A ^ { 2 } ,$ $A ^ { 3 } ,$ and $A ^ { 4 } .$ Then use the text and
a calculator to find $F _ { 2 0 } .$

4\. Suppose each "Gibonacci" number $G _ { k + 2 }$ is the average of the two previous numbers
$G _ { k + 1 }$ and $G _ { k } .$ Then $G _ { k + 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( G _ { k + 1 } + G _ { k } \right) :$

$$\begin{array}{} G _ { k + 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } G _ { k + 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } G _ { k } & & & & \\ G _ { k + 1 } & & & & & \\ G _ { k + 1 } = G _ { k + 1 } & & & & & \end{array} \left[ \begin{array}{} G _ { k + 2 } \\ G _ { k + 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} A \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} G _ { k + 1 } \\ G _ { k } \end{array} \right] .$$

(a) Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $A .$

(b) Find the limit as $n \rightarrow \infty$ of the matrices $A ^ { n } = S \Lambda ^ { n } S ^ { - 1 } .$

(c) If $G _ { 0 } = 0$ and $G _ { 1 } = 1 ,$ show that the Gibonacci numbers approach $\frac { 2 } { 3 } .$

5\. Diagonalize the Fibonacci matrix by completing $S ^ { - 1 } :$

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { A _ { 1 } } & { 2 } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { A _ { 1 } } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { A _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] \left[ \right.$$

Do the multiplication $S \Lambda ^ { k } S ^ { - 1 } \left[ \begin{array}{} 1 \\ 0 \end{array} \right]$ to find its second component. This is the kth $\mathrm { F i }$
bonacci number $F _ { k } = \left( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { k } \text { th } _ { 2 } ^ { k } \right) / \left( \text { a } _ { 1 } \text { th } _ { 2 } \right) .$

and

combination of $\lambda _ { 1 } ^ { k }$ and $2 4$ satisfies the rule. The combination $F _ { k } = \left( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { k } - \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { k } \right) / \left( \lambda _ { 1 } - \right.$

7\. Lucas started with $L _ { 0 } = 2$ and $L _ { 1 } = 1 .$ The rule $L _ { k + 2 } = L _ { k + 1 } + L _ { k }$ is the same, so $A$
is still Fibonacci's matrix. Add its eigenvectors $x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } :$

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} \lambda _ { 1 } \\ 1 \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array}{} \lambda _ { 2 } \\ 1 \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( 1 + \sqrt { 5 } \right) \\ 1 \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array}{} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( 1 - \sqrt { 5 } \right) \\ 1 \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} 1 \\ 2 \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} L _ { 1 } \\ L _ { 0 } \end{array} \right] .$$

Multiplying by $A ^ { k } ,$ the second component is $L _ { k } = \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { k } + \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { k } .$ Compute the Lucas
number $L _ { 1 0 }$ slowly by $L _ { k + 2 } = L _ { k + 1 } + L _ { k } ,$ and compute approximately by $2 1 0 .$

13\. (a) In what range of $a$ and $b$ is the following equation a Markov process?

### 5.4 Differential Equations and $e ^ { A t }$

Wherever you find a system of equations, rather than a single equation, matrix theory
has a part to play. For difference equations, the solution $u _ { k } = A ^ { k } u _ { 0 }$ depended on the owen
of $A .$ For differential equations, the solution $u \left( t \right) = e ^ { A t } u \left( 0 \right)$ depends on the exponential
of $A .$ To define this exponential. and to understand it, we turn right away to an example:

$$\begin{array}{} \text { Differential equation } \quad \frac { d u } { d t } = A u = \left[ \begin{array}{} - 2 1 \\ 1 - 2 \end{array} \right] u . \end{array}$$
(1)

The first step is always to find the eigenvalues (11 and -3) and the eigenvectors:

$$A \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left( - 1 \right) \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right]$$
$$A \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { - 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left( - 3 \right) \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { - 1 } \end{array} \right]$$
and

Then several approaches lead to $u \left( t \right) .$ Probably the best is to match the general solution
to the initial vector $u \left( 0 \right)$ att $= 0 .$

The general solution is a combination of pure exponential solutions. These are so-
lutions of the special form $c e ^ { 2 t } x ,$ where $2$ is an eigenvalue of $A$ and $x$ is its eigenvec-
tor. These pure solutions satisfy the differential equation, since $d / d t \left( c e ^ { \lambda t } x \right) = A \left( c e ^ { 2 t } x \right) .$
(They were our introduction to eigenvalues at the start of the chapter.) In this 2 by 2
example, there are two pure exponentials to be combined:


## Solution

$$u \left( t \right) = c _ { 1 } e ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } t } x _ { 1 } + c _ { 2 } e ^ { \lambda _ { 2 } t } x _ { 2 } \quad \mathrm { o r } \quad u = \left[ \begin{array}{} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & - 1 \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} e ^ { - t } \\ e ^ { - 3 t } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} c _ { 1 } \\ c _ { 2 } \end{array} \right] .$$
(2)

At time zero, when the exponentials are $e ^ { 0 } = 1 ,$ $u \left( 0 \right)$ determines $c _ { 1 }$ and $c _ { 2 } :$

$$\begin{array}{} { \text { Initial condition } \quad u \left( 0 \right) = c _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + c _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & - 1 \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} c _ { 1 } \\ c _ { 2 } \end{array} \right] = S c } \end{array}$$

You recognize $S ,$ the matrix of eigenvectors. The constants $c = S ^ { - 1 } u \left( 0 \right)$ are the same as
they were for difference equations. Substituting them back into equation (2), the solution

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="297" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.4 Differential Equations and $e ^ { A i }$" -->

is

$$u \left( t \right) = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { e ^ { - t } } & { } \\ { } & { e ^ { - 3 t } } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { c _ { 1 } } \\ { c _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] = S \left[ \begin{array}{} { e ^ { - t } } \\ { e ^ { - 3 t } } \end{array} \right] S ^ { - 1 } u \left( 0 \right) .$$
(3)

Here is the fundamental formula of this section: $S e ^ { \Lambda t } S ^ { - 1 } u \left( 0 \right)$ solves the differential
equation, just as $S \Lambda ^ { k } S$ ${ } ^ { 1 } u _ { 0 }$ solved the difference equation:

$$u \left( t \right) = S e ^ { \Lambda t } S ^ { - 1 } u \left( 0 \right) \quad \mathrm { w i t h } \quad \Lambda = \left[ \begin{array}{} - 1 & \\ - 3 & \end{array} \right] \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad e ^ { \Lambda t } = \left[ \begin{array}{} e ^ { - t } & \\ & e ^ { - 3 t } \end{array} \right] .$$
(4)

There are two more things to be done with this example. One is to complete the
mathematics, by giving a direct definition of the exponential of a matrix. The other
is to give a physical interpretation of the equation and its solution. It is the kind of
differential equation that has useful applications.

The exponential of a diagonal matrix $\Lambda$ is easy; $e ^ { \Lambda }$ just has the $n$ numbers $e ^ { 2 t }$ on
the diagonal. For a general matrix $A ,$ the natural idea is to imitate the power series
$e ^ { x } = 1 + x + x ^ { 2 } / 2 ! + x ^ { 3 } / 3 ! + \cdots .$ If we replace $x$ by $A t$ and $1 \quad b y \quad I ,$ this sum is an $n$ by $n$
$\mathrm { m a t r i x } :$

$$e ^ { A t } = I + A t + \frac { \left( A t \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 ! } + \frac { \left( A t \right) ^ { 3 } } { 3 ! } + \cdots .$$
(5)

The series always converges, and its sum $e ^ { A t }$ has the right properties:

$$\frac { d } { d t } \left( e ^ { A t } \right) = A e ^ { A t } .$$
(As) (Ar) =(e)(+), (et)(e-Ar)=I, and (6
(6)

From the last one, $u \left( t \right) = e ^ { A t } u \left( 0 \right)$ solves the differential equation. This solution must
be the same as the form $S e ^ { \Lambda t } S ^ { - 1 } u \left( 0 \right)$ used for computation. To prove directly that those
solutions agree, remember that each power $\left( S \Lambda S ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { k }$ telescopes into $A ^ { k } = S \Lambda ^ { k } S ^ { - 1 }$ (be-
cause $S ^ { - 1 }$ cancels S). The whole exponential is diagonalized by $S :$

$$e ^ { A t } = I + S \Lambda S ^ { - 1 } t + \frac { S \Lambda ^ { 2 } S ^ { - 1 } t ^ { 2 } } { 2 ! } + \frac { S \Lambda ^ { 3 } S ^ { - 1 } t ^ { 3 } } { 3 ! } + \cdots .$$
$$= S \left( I + \Lambda t + \frac { \left( \Lambda t \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 ! } + \frac { \left( \Lambda t \right) ^ { 3 } } { 3 ! } + \cdots \right) S ^ { - 1 } = S e ^ { \Lambda t } S ^ { - 1 } .$$

Example 1. In equation $\left( 1 \right) ,$ the exponential of $A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]$ has $\Lambda = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { - 3 } \end{array} \right] :$

$$e ^ { A t } = S e ^ { \Lambda t } S ^ { - 1 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & - 1 \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} e ^ { - t } \\ e ^ { - 3 t } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & - 1 \end{array} \right] ^ { - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \begin{array}{} e ^ { - t } + e ^ { - 3 t } & e ^ { - t } - e ^ { - 3 t } \\ e ^ { - t } - e ^ { - 3 t } & e ^ { - t } + e ^ { - 3 t } \end{array} \right] .$$

Att $= 0$ we get $e ^ { 0 } = I .$ The infinite series $e ^ { A t }$ gives the answer for all t, but a series can be
hard to compute. The form $S e ^ { \Lambda t } S ^ { - 1 }$ gives the same answer when $A$ can be diagonalized;
it requires $n$ independent eigenvectors in $S .$ This simpler form leads to a combination of
$n$ exponentials $e ^ { 2 t } x$ e^tx-which is the best solution of all:

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="298" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 5 Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors" -->

5L If $A$ can be diagonalized, $A = S \Lambda S ^ { - 1 } ,$ then $d u / d t = A u$ has the solution

$$u \left( t \right) = e ^ { A t } u \left( 0 \right) = S e ^ { \Lambda t } S ^ { - 1 } u \left( 0 \right) .$$
(7)

The columns of $S$ are the eigenvectors $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ of $A .$ Multiplying gives

u(t) = x1 ... xn

.

.

.

edit
s-1u(0)

eInt
(8)
$$= c _ { 1 } e ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } t } x _ { 1 } + \cdots + c _ { n } e ^ { \lambda _ { n } t } x _ { n } = c o m b i n a t i o n o f e ^ { \lambda t } x .$$

The constants $c _ { i }$ that match the initial conditions $u \left( 0 \right)$ are $c = S ^ { - 1 } u \left( 0 \right) .$

This gives a complete analogy with difference equations and $S \Lambda S ^ { - 1 } u _ { 0 } .$ In both cases
we assumed that $A$ could be diagonalized. since otherwise it has fewer than $n$ eigenvec-
tors and we have not found enough special solutions. The missing Solutions do exist,
but they are more complicated than pure exponentials $e ^ { 2 t } x .$ They involve "generalized
eigenvectors" and factors $t e ^ { 2 t } .$ (To compute this defective case we can use the Jor-
dan form in Appendix $\mathrm { B } ,$ and find $e ^ { J t }$ .) The formula $u \left( t \right) = e ^ { A t } u \left( 0 \right)$ remains completely
correct.

The matrix $e ^ { A i }$ is never singular. One proof is to look at its eigenvalues; if $2$ is an
eigenvalue of $A ,$ then $e ^ { 2 t }$ is the corresponding eigenvalue of eAt-and $e ^ { 2 t }$ can never be
zero. Another approach is to compute the determinant of the exponential:

$$d e t e ^ { A t } = e ^ { 2 _ { 1 } t } e ^ { \lambda _ { 2 } t } \cdots e ^ { \lambda _ { n } t } = e ^ { \text { trace } \left( A t \right) } .$$
(9)

Quick proof that $e ^ { A t }$ is invertible: Just recognize $e ^ { - A t }$ as its inverse.

This invertibility is fundamental for differential equations. If $n$ solutions are linearly
independent at $t = 0 ,$ they remain linearly independent forever. If the initial vectors are
$v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { n } ,$ we can put the solutions $e ^ { A t } v$ into a matrix:

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} e ^ { A t } v _ { 1 } & \cdots & e ^ { A t } v _ { n } \end{array} \right] = e ^ { A t } \left[ \begin{array}{} v _ { 1 } & \cdots & v _ { n } \end{array} \right] .$$

The determinant of the left-hand side is the Wronskian. It never becomes zero, because
it is the product of two nonzero determinants. Both matrices on the right-hand side are
invertible.

Remark. Not all differential equations come to us as a first-order system $d u / d t = A u .$
We may start from a single equation of higher order, $y ^ { \prime \prime \prime }$ $y ^ { \prime \prime \prime } - 3 y ^ { \prime \prime } + 2 y ^ { \prime } = 0 .$ To convert
to a 3 by 3 system, introduce $v = y ^ { \prime }$ and $w = v ^ { \prime }$ as additional unknowns along $\mathrm { w i t h } v$
itself. Then these two equations combine with the original one to give $u ^ { \prime } = A u :$

$$y ^ { \prime } = v$$
$$v ^ { \prime } = w$$
$$w ^ { \prime } = 3 w - 2 v$$
$$u ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { - 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { y } \\ { v } \\ { w } \end{array} \right] = A u .$$
or

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="299" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.4 Differential Equations and $e ^ { A i }$" -->

We are back to a first-order system. The problem can be solved two ways. In a course
on differential equations, you would substitute $y = e ^ { x t }$ into $y ^ { \prime \prime \prime } - 3 y ^ { \prime \prime } + 2 y ^ { \prime } = 0 :$

$$\left( \lambda ^ { 3 } - 3 \lambda ^ { 2 } + 2 \lambda \right) e ^ { \lambda t } = 0 \quad \text { or } \quad 2 \left( \lambda - 1 \right) \left( \lambda - 2 \right) e ^ { 2 t } = 0 .$$
(10)

The three pure exponential solutions are $y = e ^ { 0 t } ,$ $y = e ^ { t } ,$ and $y = e ^ { 2 t } .$ No eigenvectors are
involved. In a linear algebra course, we find the eigenvalues of $A :$

$$\mathrm { d e t } \left( A - 2 I \right) = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 2 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { - 2 } & { I } \\ { 0 } & { - 2 } & { 3 - \lambda } \end{array} \right] = - 2 ^ { 3 } + 3 A ^ { 2 } - 2 A = 0 .$$
(11)

Equations (10) and (11) are the same! The same three exponents appear: $\lambda = 0 ,$ $2 =$ 1,
and $2 = 2 .$ This is a general rule which makes the two methods consistent; the growth
rates of the solutions stay fixed when the equations change form. It seems to us that
solving the third-order equation is quicker.

The physical significance of $d u / d t = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right] u$ is easy to explain and at the same
time genuinely important. This differential equation describes a process of diffusion.
Divide an infinite pipe into four segments (Figure 5.1). $\mathrm { A t }$ time $t = 0 ,$ the middle seg-
ments contain concentrations $v \left( 0 \right)$ and $w \left( 0 \right)$ of a chemical. At each time t, the diffusion
rate between two adjacent segments is the difference in concentrations. Within each
segment, the concentration remains uniform (zero in the infinite segments). The process
is continuous in time but discrete in space; the unknowns are $v \left( t \right)$ and $w \left( t \right)$ in the two
inner segments $S _ { 1 }$ and $S _ { 2 } .$

The concentration $v \left( t \right)$ in $S _ { 1 }$ is changing in two ways. There is diffusion into $S _ { 0 } ,$ and
into or out of $S _ { 2 } .$ The net rate of change is $d v / d t ,$ and $d w / d t$ is similar:

Flow rate into S1
Flow rate into S2

$\frac { d v } { d t } = \left( w - v \right) + \left( 0 - v \right)$
$\frac { d w } { d t } = \left( 0 - w \right) + \left( v - w \right) .$

This law of diffusion exactly matches our example $d u / d t = A u :$

u=
L
W
V

and

$$\frac { d u } { d t } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 2 v + w } \\ { v - 2 w } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right] u$$

The eigenvalues -1 and $- 3$ will govern the solution. They give the rate at which the
concentrations decay, and $2 1$ is the more important because only an exceptional set of

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="300" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 5 Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors" -->

starting conditions can lead to "superdecay" at the rate $e ^ { - 3 t } ,$ In fact, those conditions
must come from the eigenvector $\left( 1 , - 1 \right) .$ If the experiment admits only nonnegative
concentrations, superdecay is impossible and the limiting rate must be $e ^ { - t }$ . The solution
that decays at this slower rate corresponds to the eigenvector $\left( 1 , 1 \right) .$ Therefore the two
concentrations will become nearly equal (typical for diffusion) ast $t \rightarrow \infty .$

One more comment on this example: It is a discrete approximation, with only two
unknowns, to the continuous diffusion described by this partial differential equation:


## Heat equation

$$\frac { \partial u } { \partial t } = \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } u } { \partial x ^ { 2 } }$$

That heat equation is approached by dividing the pipe into smaller and smaller segments,
of length $1 / N .$ The discrete system $\mathrm { w i t h } N$ unknowns is governed by

(12)

This is the finite difference matrix with the 1, -2, 1 pattern. The right side $A u$ approaches
the second derivative $d ^ { 2 } u / d x ^ { 2 } ,$ after a scaling factor $N ^ { 2 }$ comes from the flow problem.
In the limit as $N \rightarrow \infty ,$ we reach the heat equation $\partial u / \partial t = \partial ^ { 2 } u / \partial x ^ { 2 } .$ Its solutions
are still combinations of pure exponentials, but now there are infinitely many. Instead
of eigenvectors from $A x = A x ,$ we have eigenfunctions from $d ^ { 2 } u / d x ^ { 2 } = h u .$ Those are
$u \left( x \right) = \sin n \pi x$ with $2 = - n ^ { 2 } \pi ^ { 2 } .$ Then the solution to the heat equation is

$$u \left( t \right) = \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } c _ { n } e ^ { - n ^ { 2 } \pi ^ { 2 } t } \sin n \pi x .$$

The constants $c _ { n }$ are determined by the initial condition. The novelty is that the eigen-
vectors are functions $u \left( x \right) ,$ because the problem is continuous and not discrete.


### stability of differential equations

Just as for difference equations. the eigenvalues decide how $u \left( t \right)$ behaves as $t \rightarrow \infty .$
As long as $A$ can be diagonalized, there will be $n$ pure exponential solutions to the
differential equation, and any specific solution $u \left( t \right)$ is some combination

$$u \left( t \right) = S e ^ { \Lambda t } S ^ { - 1 } u _ { 0 } = c _ { 1 } e ^ { g l _ { 1 } t } x _ { 1 } + \cdots + c _ { n } e ^ { g l _ { n } t } x _ { n } .$$

Stability is governed by those factors $e ^ { g l _ { i } t } .$ If they all approach zero, then $u \left( t \right)$ approaches
zero: if they all stay bounded, then $u \left( t \right)$ stays bounded; if one of them blows up, then
except for very special starting conditions the solution will blow up. Furthermore, the
size of $e ^ { 2 t }$ depends only on the real part of $2 .$ It is only the real parts of the eigenvalues
that govern stability: If $\lambda = a + i b ,$ then

$$e ^ { 2 t } = e ^ { a t } e ^ { i b t } = e ^ { a t } \left( \cos b t + i \sin b t \right) \quad \text { and the magnitude is } \quad | e ^ { \lambda t } | = e ^ { a t } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="301" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.4 Differential Equations and $e ^ { A i }$" -->

This decays for $a < 0 ,$ $\mathrm { i t }$ is constant for $a = 0 ,$ and it explodes for $a > 0 .$ The imaginary
part is producing oscillations, but the amplitude comes from the real part.

5M The differential equation $d u / d t = A u$ is

stable and $e ^ { A t } \rightarrow 0$ whenever all $\mathrm { R e } \lambda _ { i } < 0 ,$

neutrally stable when all $\mathrm { R e } \lambda _ { i } \leq 0$ and $\mathrm { R e } \lambda _ { 1 } = 0 ,$ and

unstable and $e ^ { A t }$ is unbounded if any eigenvalue has $\mathrm { R e } \lambda _ { i } > 0 .$

In some texts the condition $\mathrm { R e } \lambda < 0$ is called asymptotic stability, because it guarantees
decay for large times t. Our argument depended on having $n$ pure exponential solutions,
but even if $A$ is not diagonalizable (and there are terms like $t e ^ { 2 t }$ the result is still true:
$A l l$ solutions approach zero if and only if all eigenvalues have $\mathrm { R e } \lambda < 0 .$

Stability is especially easy to decide for a 2 by 2 system (which is very common in
applications). The equation is

$$\frac { d u } { d t } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { a } & { b } \\ { c } & { d } \end{array} \right] u$$

and we need to know when both eigenvalues of that matrix have negative real parts.
(Note again that the eigenvalues can be complex numbers.) The stability tests are


<table>
<tr>
<td>$\mathrm { R e } \lambda _ { 1 } < 0$</td>
<td>The trace $a + d$ must be negative.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$\mathrm { R e } \lambda _ { 2 } < 0$</td>
<td>The determinant $a d \quad - \quad b c$ must be positive.</td>
</tr>
</table>


When the eigenvalues are real, those tests guarantee them to be negative. Their product
is the determinant; it is positive when the eigenvalues have the same sign. Their sum is
the trace; it is negative when both eigenvalues are negative.

When the eigenvalues are a complex pair $x \pm i y ,$ the tests still succeed. The trace
is their sum $2 x$ (which is $<$ 0) and the determinant is $\left( x + i y \right) \left( x - i y \right) = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } > 0 .$
Figure 5.2 shows the one stable quadrant, trace $< 0$ and determinant $> 0 .$ It also shows
the parabolic boundary line between real and complex eigenvalues. The reason for the
parabola is in the quadratic equation for the eigenvalues:

$$\mathrm { d e t } \left[ \begin{array}{} { a - \lambda } & { b } \\ { c } & { d - \lambda } \end{array} \right] = \lambda ^ { 2 } - \left( \mathrm { t r a c e } \right) A + \left( \mathrm { d e t } \right) = 0 .$$
(13)

The quadratic formula for $2$ leads to the parabola $\left( \mathrm { t r a c e } \right) ^ { 2 } = 4 \left( \mathrm { d e t } \right) :$

$$A _ { 1 } \text { and } A _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \text { trace } \pm \sqrt { \left( \text { trace } \right) ^ { 2 } - 4 \left( \text { det } \right) } \right] .$$
(14)

Above the parabola, the number under the square root is negative-so $2$ is not real. On
the parabola, the square root is zero and $2$ is repeated. Below the parabola the square
roots are real. Every symmetric matrix has real eigenvalues, since if $b = c ,$ then

$$\left( \text { trace } \right) ^ { 2 } - 4 \left( d e t \right) = \left( a + d \right) ^ { 2 } - 4 \left( a d - b ^ { 2 } \right) = \left( a - d \right) ^ { 2 } + 4 b ^ { 2 } \geq 0 .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="302" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 5 Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors" -->

<figcaption>Figure 5.2: Stability and instability regions for a 2 by 2 matrix.</figcaption>

</figure>


For complex eigenvalues, $b$ and $c$ have opposite signs and are sufficiently large.

Example 2. One from each quadrant: only $\# 2$ is stable:

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] \quad \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right] \quad \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right] \quad \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right]$$

On the boundaries of the second quadrant, the equation is neutrally stable. On the hori-
zontal axis, one eigenvalue is zero (because the determinant is $\left. 2 1 2 2 = 0 \right) .$ On the vertical
axis above the origin, both eigenvalues are purely imaginary (because the trace is Zero).
Crossing those axes are the two ways that stability is lost.

The $n$ by $n$ case is more difficult. A test for $\mathrm { R e } \lambda _ { i } < 0$ came from Routh and Hurwitz,
who found a series of inequalities on the entries $a _ { i j } .$ I do not think this approach is
much good for a large matrix; the computer can probably find the eigenvalues with more
certainty than it can test these inequalities. Lyapunov's idea was to find a weighting
matrix $W$ so that the weighted length $\| W u \left( t \right) \|$ is always decreasing. If there exists
such a $W ,$ then $| W u | |$ will decrease steadily to zero, and after a few ups and downs u
must get there too (stability). The real value of Lyapunov's method is for a nonlinear
equation-then stability can be proved without knowing a formula for $u \left( t \right) .$

Example 3. $d u / d t = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { - 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] u$ sends $u \left( t \right)$ around a circle, starting from $u \left( 0 \right) = \left( 1 , 0 \right) .$
Since trace $= 0$ and $\mathrm { d e t } = 1$ 1, we have purely imaginary eigenvalues:

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { - 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right] = A ^ { 2 } + 1 = 0 \quad \text { so } \quad A = + i \text { and } - i .$$

The eigenvectors are $\left( 1 , - i \right)$ and $\left( 1 , i \right) .$ and the solution is

$$u \left( t \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { i t } \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { - i } \end{array} \right] + \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - i t } \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { i } \end{array} \right]$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="303" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.4 Differential Equations and $e ^ { A i }$" -->

That is correct but not beautiful. By substituting $\cos t \pm i \sin t$ for $e ^ { i l }$ and $e ^ { - i t } ,$ real num-
bers will reappear: The circling solution is $u \left( t \right) = \left( \cos t , \sin t \right) .$

Starting from a different $u \left( 0 \right) = \left( a , b \right) ,$ the solution $u \left( t \right)$ ends up as

$$u \left( t \right) = \left[ \begin{array}{} { a \cos t - b \sin t } \\ { b \cos t + a \sin t } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { \cos t } & { - \sin t } \\ { \sin t } & { \cos t } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { a } \\ { b } \end{array} \right]$$
(15)

There we have something important! The last matrix is multiplying $u \left( 0 \right) ,$ so it must be
the exponential $e ^ { A t } .$ (Remember that $u \left( t \right) = e ^ { A t } u \left( 0 \right)$ That matrix of cosines and sines
is our leading example of an orthogonal matrix. The columns have length 1, their inner
product is zero, and we have a confirmation of a wonderful fact:

If $A$ is skew-symmetric $\left( A ^ { T } = - A \right)$ then $e ^ { A i }$ is an orthogonal matrix.

$A ^ { T } = - A$ gives a conservative system. No energy is lost in damping or diffusion:

$$A ^ { \mathrm { T } } = - A , \quad \left( e ^ { A t } \right) ^ { \mathrm { T } } = e ^ { - A t } , \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \| e ^ { A t } u \left( 0 \right) \| = \| u \left( 0 \right) \| .$$

That last equation expresses an essential property of orthogonal matrices. When they
multiply a vector, the length is not changed. The vector $u \left( 0 \right)$ is just rotated, and that
describes the solution to $d u / d t = A u :$ It goes around in a circle.

In this very unusual case, $e ^ { A t }$ can also be recognized directly from the infinite series.
Note that $A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { - 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]$ has $A ^ { 2 } = - I ,$ and use this in the series for $e ^ { A t } :$

$$I + A t + \frac { \left( A t \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { \left( A t \right) ^ { 3 } } { 6 } + \cdots = \left[ \left( \begin{array}{} 1 - \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \cdots \\ \left( t - \frac { t ^ { 3 } } { 6 } + \cdots \right) \\ \left( t - \frac { t ^ { 3 } } { 6 } + \cdots \right) \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} - t + \frac { t ^ { 3 } } { 6 } - \cdots \\ \left( 1 - \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \cdots \right) \end{array} \right) \right]$$
$$= \left[ \begin{array}{} { \cos t } & { - \sin t } \\ { \sin t } & { \cos t } \end{array} \right]$$

Example 4. The diffusion equation is stable:
$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]$
1
has $2 = - \quad 1$ and $2 = - \quad 3 .$

Example 5. If we close off the infinite segments, nothing can escape:

$$\frac { d u } { d t } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] u$$
$$d v / d t = w - v$$
$$d w / d t = v - w .$$

This is a continuous Markov process. Instead of moving every year, the particles move
every instant. Their total number $v + w$ is constant. That comes from adding the two
equations on the right-hand side: the derivative of $v + w$ is zero.

A discrete Markov matrix has its column sums equal to $2 _ { \max } = 1 .$ A continuous
Markov matrix, for differential equations, has its column sums equal to $A _ { \max } = 0 .$ $A$ is
a discrete Markov matrix if and only if $B = A \quad - \quad I$ is a continuous Markov matrix. The

(b) $\omega _ { 2 } = \sqrt { 3 } ,$ $x _ { 2 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} 1 \\ - 1 \end{array} \right]$

</figure>


steady state for both is the eigenvector for $2 _ { m a x } .$ It is multiplied by $1 ^ { k } = 1$ in difference
equations and by $e ^ { 0 t } = 1$ in differential equations, and it doesn't move.

In the example, the steady state has $v =$ w.

Example 6. In nuclear engineering, a reactor is called critical when it is neutrally
stable; the fission balances the decay. Slower fission makes it stable, or subcritical, and
eventually it runs down. Unstable fission is a bomb.


#### Second-Order Equations

The laws of diffusion led to a first-order system $d u / d t = A u .$ So do a lot of other appli-
cations, in chemistry, in biology, and elsewhere, but the most important law of physics
does not. It is Newton's law $F = m a ,$ and the acceleration a is a second derivative. In-
ertial terms produce second-order equations (we have to solve $d ^ { 2 } u / d t ^ { 2 } = A u$ instead of
$\left. d u / d t = A u \right) ,$ and the goal is to understand how this switch to second derivatives alters
the solution3. It is optional in linear algebra, but not in physics.

The comparison will be perfect if we keep the same $A :$

$$\frac { d ^ { 2 } u } { d t ^ { 2 } } = A u = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right] u$$
(16)

Two initial conditions get the system started-the "displacement" $u \left( 0 \right)$ and the "veloc-
ity" $u ^ { \prime } \left( 0 \right) .$ To match these conditions, there will be $2 n$ pure exponential solutions.

Suppose we use $@$ rather than $2 ,$ and write these special solutions as $u = e ^ { i \infty t } x .$ Sub-
stituting this exponential into the differential equation, it must satisfy

$$\frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } \left( e ^ { i o t } x \right) = A \left( e ^ { i o t } x \right) , \quad \text { or } \quad - w ^ { 2 } x = A x .$$
(17)

The vector $x$ must be an eigenvector of $A ,$ exactly as before. The corresponding eigen-
value is now $- \infty ^ { 2 } ,$ so the frequency $@$ is connected to the decay rate $2$ by the law

5Fourth derivatives are also possible, in the bending of beams, but nature seems to resist going higher than four.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="305" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.4 Differential Equations and $e ^ { A i }$" -->

$- a ^ { 2 } = 2 .$ Every special solution $e ^ { i t } x$ of the first-order equation leads to two special
solutions $e ^ { i o t } x$ of the second-order equation. and the two exponents are $\infty = \pm \sqrt { - 2 } .$
This breaks down only when $2 = 0 ,$ $w h i c h$ has just one square root; if the eigenvector is
x, the two special solutions are $\chi$ and $t x .$

For a genuine diffusion matrix, the eigenvalues $2$ are all negative and the frequencies
$@$ are all real: Pure diffusion is converted into pure oscillation. The factors $e ^ { i 0 l }$ produce
neutral stability, the solution neither grows or decays, and the total energy stays precisely
constant. It just keeps passing around the system. The general solution to $d ^ { 2 } u / d t ^ { 2 } = A u ,$
if $A$ has negative eigenvalues $\lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n }$ and if $\infty _ { j } = \sqrt { - \lambda _ { j } } ,$ is

$$u \left( t \right) = \left( c _ { 1 } e ^ { i o _ { 1 } t } + d _ { 1 } e ^ { - o _ { 1 } t } \right) x _ { 1 } + c d o t s + \left( c _ { n } e ^ { i o _ { n } t } + d _ { n } e ^ { - o _ { n } t } \right) x _ { n } .$$
(18)

As always, the constants are found from the initial conditions. This is easier to do (at the
expense of one extra formula) by switching from oscillating exponentials to the more
familiar sine and cosine:

$$u \left( t \right) = \left( a _ { 1 } \cos \alpha _ { 1 } t + b _ { 1 } \sin a _ { 1 } t \right) x _ { 1 } + \cdots + \left( a _ { n } \cos a _ { n } t + b _ { n } \sin a _ { n } t \right) x _ { n } .$$
(19)

The initial displacement $u \left( 0 \right)$ is easy to keep separate: $t = 0$ means that $\sin \alpha t = 0$ and
$\cos 6 0 t = 1 ,$ leaving only

$$u \left( 0 \right) = a _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdots + a _ { n } x _ { n } , \quad \mathrm { o r } \quad u \left( 0 \right) = S a , \quad \mathrm { o r } \quad a = S ^ { - 1 } u \left( 0 \right) .$$

Then differentiating $u \left( t \right)$ and setting $t = 0 .$ the b's are determined by the initial velocity:
$u ^ { \prime } \left( 0 \right) = b _ { 1 } a _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdots + b _ { n } a _ { n } x _ { n } .$ Substituting the a's and b's into the formula for $u \left( t \right) ,$ the
equation is solved.

The matrix $A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]$ has $2 _ { 1 } = - 1$ and $x _ { 2 } = - 3 .$ The frequencies are $\omega _ { 1 } = 1$ and
$a _ { 2 } = \sqrt { 3 } .$ If the system starts from rest, $u ^ { \prime } \left( 0 \right) = 0 ,$ the terms in $b \sin a t$ will disappear:

Solution from u(0) =
0
1

$$u \left( t \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cos t \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \cos \sqrt { 3 } t \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { - 1 } \end{array} \right]$$

Physically, two masses are connected to each other and to stationary wails by three
identical springs (Figure 5.3). The first mass is held at $v \left( 0 \right) = 1 ,$ the second mass is held
at $w \left( 0 \right) = 0 ,$ and at $t = 0$ we let go. Their motion $u \left( t \right)$ becomes an average of two pure
oscillations, corresponding to the two eigenvectors. In the first mode $x _ { 1 } = \left( 1 , 1 \right) ,$ the
masses move together and the spring in the middle is never stretched (Figure 5.3a). The
frequency $\omega _ { 1 } = 1$ is the same as for a single spring and a single mass. In the faster mode
$x _ { 2 } = \left( 1 , - 1 \right)$ with frequency $\sqrt { 3 } ,$ the masses move oppositely but with equal speeds. The
general solution is a combination of these two normal modes. Our particular solution is
half of each.

As time goes on, the motion is "almost periodic." If the ratio $\infty / a b$ had been a
fraction like $2 / 3 ,$ the masses would eventually return to $u \left( 0 \right) = \left( 1 , 0 \right)$ and begin again.
$\mathrm { A }$ combination of $\sin 2 t$ and $\sin 3 t$ would have a period of $2 \pi .$ But $\sqrt { 3 }$ is irrational. The

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="306" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 5 Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors" -->

best we can say is that the masses will come arbitrarily close to $\left( 1 , 0 \right)$ and also $\left( 0 , 1 \right) .$
Like a billiard ball bouncing forever on a perfectly smooth table, the total energy is fixed.
Sooner or later the masses come near any state with this energy.

Again we cannot leave the problem without drawing a parallel to the continuous case.
As the discrete masses and springs merge into a solid rod, the "second differences" given
by the 1, -2, 1 matrix $A$ turn into second derivatives. This limit is described by the
celebrated wave equation $\partial ^ { 2 } u / \partial t ^ { 2 } = \partial ^ { 2 } u / \partial x ^ { 2 } .$

##### 5.5 Complex Matrices

It is no longer possible to work only with real vectors and real matrices In the first half of
this book, when the basic problem was $A x \quad - \quad b ,$ the solution was real when $A$ and $b$ were
real. Complex numbers could have been permitted. but would have contributed nothing
new. Now we cannot avoid them. A real matrix has real coefficients in $\mathrm { d e t } \left( A - 2 I \right) ,$ but
the eigenvalues (as in rotations) may be complex.

We now introduce the space $\mathrm { C } ^ { n }$ of vectors with $n$ complex components. Addition and
matrix multiplication follow the same rules as before. Length is computed differently.
The old way, the vector in $C ^ { 2 }$ with components $\left( 1 , i \right)$ would have zero length: $1 ^ { 2 } + i ^ { 2 } = 0 ,$
not good. The correct length squared is $1 ^ { 2 } + | i | ^ { 2 } = 2 .$

This change to $| | x | | ^ { 2 } = | x _ { 1 } | ^ { 2 } + \cdots + | x _ { n } | ^ { 2 }$ forces a whole series of other changes. The
inner product, the transpose, the definitions of symmetric and orthogonal matrices, all
need to be modified for complex numbers. The new definitions coincide with the old
when the vectors and matrices are real. We have listed these changes in a table at the
end of the section. and we explain them as we go.

That table virtually amounts to a dictionary for translating real into complex. We
hope it will be useful to the reader. We particularly want to find out about symmetric
matrices and Hermitian matrices: Where are their eigenvalues, and what is special
about their eigenvectors? For practical purposes, those are the most important questions
in the theory of eigenvalues. We call attention in advance to the answers:

1\. Every symmetric matrix (and Hermitian matrix) has real eigenvalues.

2\. Its eigenvectors can be chosen to be orthonormal.

Strangely, to prove that the eigenvalues are real we begin with the opposite possibility-
and that takes us to complex numbers, complex vectors, and complex matrices.


## Complex Numbers and Their Conjugates

Probably the reader has already met complex numbers; a review is easy to give. The
important ideas are the complex conjugate $\bar { X }$ and the absolute value $| x | .$ Everyone knows
that whatever $\dot { l }$ is, it satisfies the equation $i ^ { 2 } = - 1 .$ It is a pure imaginary number, and
so are its multiples $i b ;$ $b$ is real. The sum $a + i b$ is a complex number, and it is plotted in
a natural way on the complex plane (Figure 5.4).

The real numbers $a$ and the imaginary numbers $i b$ are special cases of complex num-
bers; they lie on the axes. Two complex numbers are easy to add:


### Complex addition $\left( a + i b \right) + \left( c + i d \right) = \left( a + c \right) + i \left( b + d \right) .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="313" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.5 Complex Matrices" -->

<figcaption>Figure 5.4: The complex plane, with $a + i b = r e ^ { i \theta }$ and its conjugate $a - i b = r e ^ { - i \theta } .$</figcaption>

Multiplying $a + i b$ times $c + i d$ uses the rule that $i ^ { 2 } = - 1 :$

Multiplication

$$\left( a + i b \right) \left( c + i d \right) = a c + i b c + i a d + i ^ { 2 } b d$$
$$= \left( a c - b d \right) + i \left( b c + a d \right) .$$

The complex conjugate of $a + i b$ is the number $a \quad - \quad i b .$ The sign of the imaginary
part is reversed. It is the mirror image across the real axis; any real number is its own
conjugate, since $b =$ 0. The conjugate is denoted by a bar or a star: $\left( a + i b \right) ^ { * } = \bar { a + i b } =$
$a \quad - \quad i b .$ It has three important properties:

1\. The conjugate of a product equals the product of the conjugates:

$$\bar { \left( a + i b \right) \left( c + i d \right) } = \left( a c - b d \right) - i \left( b c + a d \right) = \bar { \left( a + i b \right) \left( c + i d \right) } .$$
(1)

2\. The conjugate of a sum equals the sum of the conjugates:

$$\bar { \left( a + c \right) + i \left( b + d \right) } = \left( a + c \right) - i \left( b + d \right) = \bar { \left( a + i b \right) } + \bar { \left( c + i d \right) } .$$

3\. Multiplying any $a + i b$ by its conjugate $a \quad - \quad i b$ produces a real number $a ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } :$


## Absolute value

$$\left( a + i b \right) \left( a - i b \right) = a ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } .$$
(2)

This distance $r$ is the absolute value $| a + i b | = \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } } .$

Finally, trigonometry connects the sides $a$ and $b$ to the hypotenuse r by $a = r \cos \theta$
and $b = r \sin \theta .$ Combining these two equations moves us into polar coordinates:

$$\text { Polar form } \quad a + i b = r \left( \cos \theta + i \sin \theta \right) = r e ^ { i \theta } .$$
(3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="314" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 5 Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors" -->

The most important special case is when $r =$ 1. Then $a + i b$ is $e ^ { i \theta } = \cos \theta + i \sin \theta .$ It
falls on the unit circle in the complex plane. As $\theta$ varies from 0 to $2 \pi ,$ this number $e ^ { i \theta }$
circles around zero at the constant radial distance $| e ^ { i \theta } | = \sqrt { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } = 1 .$

Example 1. $x = 3 + 4 i$ times its conjugate $x = 3 - \quad 4 i$ is the absolute value squared:

$$x = \left( 3 + 4 i \right) \left( 3 - 4 i \right) = 2 5 = | x | ^ { 2 } \quad \text { so } \quad r = | x | = 5 .$$

To divide by $3 + 4 i ,$ multiply numerator and denominator by its conjugate $3 \quad - \quad 4 i :$

$$\frac { 2 + i } { 3 + 4 i } = \frac { 2 + i } { 3 + 4 i } \frac { 3 - 4 i } { 3 - 4 i } = \frac { 1 0 - 5 i } { 2 5 }$$

In polar coordinates, multiplication and division are easy:

$r e ^ { i \theta }$ times $R e ^ { i \alpha }$ has absolute value $r R$ and angle $\theta + \alpha .$

$$r e ^ { i \theta } \text { divided by } R e ^ { i \alpha } \text { has absolute value } r / R \text { and angle } \theta - \alpha .$$


## Lengths and Transposes in the Complex Case

We return to linear algebra, and make the conversion from real to complex. By definition,
the complex vector space $\mathrm { C } ^ { n }$ contains all vectors x with n complex components:

$$\text { Complex vector } \quad x = \left[ \begin{array}{} x _ { 1 } \\ x _ { 2 } \\ \vdots \\ x _ { n } \end{array} \right] \quad \text { with components } \quad x _ { j } = a _ { j } + i b _ { j } .$$

Vectors $x$ and $y$ are still added component by component. Scalar multiplication $c x$ is
now done with complex numbers c. The vectors $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { k }$ are linearly dependent $\mathrm { i f }$
some nontrivial combination gives $c _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } + \ldots + c _ { k } v _ { k } = 0$ the $c _ { j }$ may now be complex.
The unit coordinate vectors are still in $\mathrm { C } ^ { n }$ they are still independent; and they still form
a basis. Therefore $\mathrm { C } ^ { n }$ is a complex vector space of dimension n.

In the new definition of length, each $x _ { j } ^ { 2 }$ is replaced by its modulus $| x _ { j } | ^ { 2 } :$

Length squared
and
|x|2 = 2;
y =
2+i

$| | x | | ^ { 2 } = | x _ { 1 } | ^ { 2 } + \cdots + | x _ { n } | ^ { 2 } .$
(4)

Example 2. $x = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { i } \end{array} \right]$

For real vectors there was a close connection between the length and the inner product:
$| | x | | ^ { 2 } = x ^ { T } x .$ This connection we want to preserve. The inner product must be modified
to match the new definition of length, and we conjugate the first vector in the inner
product. $x \text { by } \frac { x } { x } ,$ the inner product becomes

$$\text { Inner product } \quad \bar { x } ^ { \mathrm { T } } y = \bar { x } _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdots + \bar { x } _ { n } y _ { n } .$$
(5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="315" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.5 Complex Matrices" -->

If we take the inner product of $x = \left( 1 + 3 i , 3 i \right)$ with itself, we are back to $| | x | | ^ { 2 } :$

$$\bar { x } ^ { \mathrm { T } } x = \bar { \left( 1 + i \right) } \left( 1 + i \right) + \bar { \left( 3 i \right) } \left( 3 i \right) = 2 + 9 \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \| x \| ^ { 2 } = 1 1 .$$
Length squared

Note that $\bar { y } ^ { T } x$ is different from $\bar { x } ^ { T } y$ we have to watch the order of the vectors.

This leaves only one more change in notation, condensing two symbols into one.
Instead of a bar for the conjugate and a $\mathrm { T }$ for the transpose, those are combined into the
conjugate transpose. For vectors and matrices, a superscript $\mathrm { H }$ (or a star) combines both
operations. This matrix $\bar { A } ^ { \mathrm { T } } = A ^ { \mathrm { H } } = A ^ { * }$ is called "A Hermitian":

$$\left( A ^ { H } \right) _ { i j } = \bar { A _ { j i } } .$$
"A Hermitian" AH =A has entries
(6)

You have to listen closely to distinguish that name from the phrase ${ } ^ { * } A$ is Hermitian,"
which means that $A$ equals $A ^ { \mathrm { H } } .$ If $A$ is an $m$ by $n$ matrix, $A ^ { H } \text { is } n \text { by } m :$


### Conjugate transpose

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 2 + i } & { 3 i } \\ { 4 - i } & { 5 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 2 - i } & { 4 + i } & { 0 } \\ { - 3 i } & { 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]$$

This symbol $A ^ { H }$ gives official recognition to the fact that, with complex entries, it is
very seldom that we want only the transpose of $A .$ It is the conjugate transpose $A ^ { H }$ that
becomes appropriate, and $x ^ { H }$ is the row vector $\left[ \begin{array}{} \bar { x } _ { 1 } & \cdots & \bar { x } _ { n } \end{array} \right] .$

5N

1\. The inner product of $x$ and $y$ is $x ^ { H } y .$ Orthogonal vectors have $x ^ { H } y = 0 .$

2\. The squared length of x $X$ is $| | x | | ^ { 2 } = x ^ { H } x = | x _ { 1 } | ^ { 2 } + \cdots + | x _ { n } | ^ { 2 } .$

3\. Conjugating $\left( A B \right) ^ { T } = B ^ { T } A ^ { T }$ produces $\left( A B \right) ^ { H } = B ^ { H } A ^ { H } .$


## Hermitian Matrices

We spoke in earlier chapters about symmetric matrices: $A = A ^ { T } .$ With complex entries,
this idea of symmetry has to be extended. The right generalization is not to matrices that
equal their transpose, but to matrices that equal their conjugate transpose. These are
the Hermitian matrices, and a typical example is $A :$

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 2 } & { 3 - 3 i } \\ { 3 + 3 i } & { 5 } \end{array} \right] = A ^ { H }$$
(7)

The diagonal entries must be real; they are unchanged by conjugation. Each off-diagonal
entry is matched with its mirror image across the main diagonal, and $3 \quad - \quad 3 i$ is the conju-
gate $o f \quad 3 + 3 i .$ In every case, $a _ { i j } = \bar { a _ { j i } } .$

Our main goal is to establish three basic properties of Hermitian matrices. These
properties apply equally well to symmetric matrices. $A$ real symmetric matrix is $c e r -$
tainly Hermitian. (For real matrices there is no difference between $A ^ { T }$ and $A ^ { \mathrm { H } }$ The
eigenvalues of $A$ are real-as we now prove.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="316" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 5 Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors" -->

Property 1 If $A = A ^ { H } ,$ then for all complex vectors x, the number $x ^ { H } A x$ is real.
Every entry of $A$ contributes $x ^ { H } A x .$ Try the 2 by 2 case with $x = \left( u , v \right) :$

$$x ^ { H } A x = \left[ \begin{array}{} { \pi } & { v } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { 2 } & { 3 - 3 i } \\ { 3 + 3 i } & { 5 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { u } \\ { v } \end{array} \right]$$
$$= 2 \pi u + 5 \bar { v } v + \left( 3 - 3 i \right) \bar { u } v + \left( 3 + 3 i \right) u \bar { v }$$
$$= r e a l + r e a l + \left( s u m \quad o f \quad c o m p l e x \right.$$
conjugates).

For a proof in general. $\left( x ^ { H } A x \right) ^ { H }$ is the conjugate of the 1 by 1 matrix $x ^ { H } A x ,$ but we
actually get the same number back again: $\left( x ^ { H } A x \right) ^ { H } = x ^ { H } A ^ { H } x ^ { H H } = x ^ { H } A x .$ So that number
must be real.

Property 2 If $A = A ^ { H } ,$ every eigenvalue is real.

Proof. Suppose $A x = A x .$ The trick is to multiply by $x ^ { H } :$ $x ^ { H } A x = 2 x ^ { H } x .$ The left-hand
side is real by Property 1, and the right-hand side $x ^ { H } x = | | x | | ^ { 2 }$ is real and positive, because
$x \neq 0 .$ Therefore $\lambda = x ^ { \mathrm { H } } A x / x ^ { \mathrm { H } } x$ must be real. Our example has $2 = 8$ and $2 = -$ 1:

$$| A - 2 I | = | \begin{array}{} { 2 - 2 } & { 3 - 3 i } \\ { 3 + 3 i } & { 5 - 2 } \end{array} | = 2 ^ { 2 } - 7 2 + 1 0 - | 3 - 3 i | ^ { 2 }$$
$$= A ^ { 2 } - 7 2 - 8 = \left( 2 - 8 \right) \left( 2 + 1 \right) .$$
(8)

☐

Note. This proof of real eigenvalues looks correct for any real matrix:

$$\quad A x = 2 x \quad \text { gives } \quad x ^ { T } A x = 2 x ^ { T } x , \quad \text { so } \quad 2 = \frac { x ^ { T } A x } { x ^ { T } x } \quad \text { is real. }$$

There must be a catch: The eigenvector $x$ might be complex. It is when $A = A ^ { T }$ that
we can be sure $2$ and $x$ stay real. More than that, the eigenvectors are perpendicular:
$x ^ { T } y = 0$ in the real symmetric case and $x ^ { H } y = 0$ in the complex Hermitian case.

Property 3 Two eigenvectors of a real symmetric matrix or a Hermitian ma-
trix, if they come from different eigenvalues, are orthogonal to one another.

The proof starts with $A x = A _ { 1 } x ,$ $A y = A _ { 1 } y ,$ and $A = A ^ { H } :$

$$\left( 2 _ { 1 } x \right) ^ { H } y = \left( A x \right) ^ { H } y = x ^ { H } A y = x ^ { H } \left( A _ { 2 } y \right) .$$
(9)

The outside numbers are $2 _ { 1 } x ^ { H } y = h _ { 2 } x ^ { H } y ,$ since the A's are real. Now wc use the assump-
tion $x _ { 1 } \neq x _ { 2 } ,$ which forces the conclusion that $x ^ { H } y = 0 .$ In our example,

$$\left( A - 8 I \right) x = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 6 } & { 3 - i } \\ { 3 + 3 i } & { - 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { x _ { 1 } } \\ { x _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] , \quad x = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { 1 + i } \end{array} \right]$$
$$\left( A + I \right) y = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 3 } & { 3 - 3 i } \\ { 3 + 3 i } & { 6 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { y _ { 1 } } \\ { y _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] , \quad y = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 - i } \\ { - 1 } \end{array} \right]$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="317" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.5 Complex Matrices" -->

These two eigenvectors are orthogonal:

$$x ^ { H } y = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 - i } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 - i } \\ { - 1 } \end{array} \right] = 0$$

Of course any multiples $x / \alpha$ and $y / \beta$ are equally good as eigenvectors. MATLAB
picks $\alpha = | | x |$ and $\beta = | | y | | ,$ so that $x / \alpha$ and $y / \beta$ are unit vectors; the eigenvectors
are normalized to have length 1. They are now orthonormal. If these eigenvectors are
chosen to be the columns of $S ,$ then we have $S ^ { - 1 } A S = \Lambda$ as always. The diagonalizing
matrix can be chosen with orthonormal columns when $A = A ^ { H } .$

In case $A$ is real and symmetric, its eigenvalues are real by Property 2. Its unit
eigenvectors are orthogonal by Property 3. Those eigenvectors are also real; they solve
$\left( A - \quad A I \right) x = 0 .$ These orthonormal eigenvectors go into an orthogonal matrix $Q ,$ with
$Q ^ { T } Q = I$ and $Q ^ { T } = Q ^ { - 1 } .$ Then $S ^ { - 1 } A S = \Lambda$ becomes special-it is $Q ^ { - 1 } A Q = \Lambda$ or
$A = Q \Lambda Q ^ { - 1 } = Q \Lambda Q ^ { T } .$ We can state one of the great theorems of linear algebra:

50 A real symmetric matrix can be factored into $A = Q \Lambda Q ^ { T } .$ Its orthonormal
eigenvectors are in the orthogonal matrix $Q$ and its eigenvalues are in $\Lambda .$

In geometry or mechanics, this is the principal axis theorem. It gives the right choice
of axes for an ellipse. Those axes are perpendicular, and they point along the eigen-
vectors of the corresponding matrix. (Section 6.2 connects symmetric matrices to n-
dimensional ellipses.) In mechanics the eigenvectors give the principal directions, along
which there is pure compression or pure tension-with no shear.

In mathematics the formula $A = Q \Lambda Q ^ { T }$ is known as the spectral theorem. If we
multiply columns by rows, the matrix $A$ becomes a combination of one-dimensional
projections-which are the special matrices $x x ^ { T }$ of rank 1, multiplied by $2 :$

$$A = Q \Lambda Q ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { x _ { 1 } } & { \cdots } x _ { n } \\ & | \end{array} \right]$$
$$= 2 _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } ^ { T } + 2 _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } ^ { T } + \cdots + 2 _ { n } x _ { n } x _ { n } ^ { T } .$$
(10)

Our 2 by 2 example has eigenvalues 3 and 1:

Example 3. $A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] = 3 \left[ \begin{array}{} { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \\ { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \end{array} \right.$

The eigenvectors, with length scaled to 1, are
1
2

$$x _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \quad \text { and } \quad x _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right]$$

Then the matrices on the right-hand side are $x _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } ^ { T }$ and x2x2-columns times rows-and
they are projections onto the line through $x _ { 1 }$ and the line through $x _ { 2 } .$

All symmetric matrices are combinations of one-dimensional projections-which are
symmetric matrices of rank 1.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="318" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 5 Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors" -->

Remark. If $A$ is real and its eigenvalues happen to be real, then its eigenvectors are also
real. They solve $\left( A \quad - \quad A I \right) x = 0$ and can be computed by elimination. But they will not
be orthogonal unless $A$ is symmetric: $A = Q \Lambda Q ^ { T }$ leads to $A ^ { T } = A .$

If $A$ is real, all complex eigenvalues come in conjugate pairs: $A x = 2 x$ and $A \bar { x } = 2 \bar { x } .$
If $a + i b$ is an eigenvalue of a real matrix, so is $a \quad - \quad i b .$ (If $A = A ^ { T }$ then $b =$ 0.)

Strictly speaking, the spectral theorem $A = Q \Lambda Q ^ { T }$ has been proved only when the
eigenvalues of $A$ are distinct. Then there are certainly $n$ independent eigenvectors, and
$A$ can be safely diagonalized. Nevertheless it is true (see Section 5.6) that even with
repeated eigenvalues, a symmetric matrix still has a complete set of orthonormal eigen-
vectors. The extreme case is the identity matrix, which has $2 = 1$ repeated $n$ times-and
no shortage of eigenvectors.

To finish the complex case we need the analogue of a real orthogonal matrix-and you
can guess what happens to the requirement $Q ^ { T } Q = I .$ The transpose will be replaced by
the conjugate transpose. The condition will become $U ^ { H } U = I .$ The new letter $U$ reflects
the new name: A complex matrix with orthonormal columns is called a unitary matrix.


## Unitary Matrices

May we propose two analogies? $A$ Hermitian (or symmetric) matrix can be compared
to a real number. A unitary (or orthogonal) matrix can be compared to a number on
the unit circle-a complex number of absolute value 1. The A's are real if $A ^ { H } = A ,$ and
they are on the unit circle if $U ^ { \mathrm { H } } U = I .$ The eigenvectors can be scaled to unit length and
made orthonormal.6

Those statements are not yet proved for unitary (including orthogonal) matrices.
Therefore we go directly to the three properties of $U$ that correspond to the earlier Prop-
erties 1-3 of $A .$ Remember that $U$ has orthonormal columns:

$$\mathrm { U n i t a r y } \mathrm { m a t r i x } \quad U ^ { \mathrm { H } } U = I , \quad U U ^ { \mathrm { H } } = I , \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad U ^ { \mathrm { H } } = U ^ { - 1 } .$$

This leads directly to Property 1', that multiplication by $U$ has no effect on inner prod-
ucts, angles, or lengths. The proof is on one line, just as it was for $\varrho :$

Property $1 ^ { \prime }$ $\left( U x \right) ^ { H } \left( U y \right) = x ^ { H } U ^ { H } U y = x ^ { H } y$ and lengths are preserved by $U :$
Length unchanged
$| | U x | | ^ { 2 } = x ^ { H } U ^ { H } U x = | | x | | ^ { 2 } .$
(11)

Property $2 ^ { \prime }$ Every eigenvalue of $U$ has absolute value $| \lambda | = 1 .$

This follows directly from $U x = A x ,$ by comparing the lengths of the two sides:
$| U x | = | x |$ by Property $1 ^ { \prime } ,$ and always | $2 x | = | 2 | x | .$ Therefore | $| \lambda | = 1 .$

6Later we compare "skew-Hermitian" matrices with pure imaginary numbers, and "normal" matrices with all
complex numbers $a + i b .$ A nonnormal matrix without orthogonal eigenvectors belongs to none of these classes,
and is outside the whole analogy.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="319" -->
<!-- PageHeader="5.5 Complex Matrices" -->

Property $3 ^ { \prime }$ Eigenvectors corresponding to different eigenvalues are orthonor-
mal.

Start with $U x = A _ { 1 } x$ and $U y = A _ { 2 } y ,$ and take inner products by Property $1 ^ { \prime } :$

$$x ^ { H } y = \left( U x \right) ^ { H } \left( U y \right) = \left( A _ { 1 } x \right) ^ { H } \left( A _ { 2 } y \right) = \bar { A } _ { 1 } A _ { 2 } x ^ { H } y .$$

Comparing the left to the right, $2 _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 2 } = 1$ or $x ^ { H } y = 0 .$ But Property $2 ^ { \prime }$ is $2 _ { 1 } 2 _ { 1 } = 1 ,$ so we
cannot also have $\bar { \lambda } _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 2 } = 1 .$ Thus $x ^ { H } y = 0$ and the eigenvectors are orthogonal.

Example 4.
$U = \left[ \begin{array}{} { \cos t } & { - \sin t } \\ { \sin t } & { \cos t } \end{array} \right]$
has eigenvalues eit and $e ^ { - i t }$ .

The orthogonal eigenvectors are $x = \left( 1 , - i \right)$ and $y = \left( 1 , i \right) .$ (Remember to take conjugates
in $x ^ { H } y = 1 + i ^ { 2 } = 0$ After division by $\sqrt { 2 }$ they are orthonormal.

Here is the most important unitary matrix by far.

Example 5. U =

Fourier matrix
